title: ACEs, Problematic Social Media Use, and Emotional/Behavioral Dysregulation Among ABCD
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data from baseline through 3-year follow-up (Data Release 5.1, 2016-2022); secondary analysis of adolescents ages 10-14 years
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data; secondary analysis of adolescent and parent data collected from baseline through 3-year follow-up in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Problematic social media use (PSMU) measured via Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ); SMAQ sum score (range 6-36) assessed social media addiction symptoms
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Measures problematic social media use (PSMU) via Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ), adapted from Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale; item example: 'I feel the need to use social media sites/apps more and more'
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Mixed-effects linear regression testing association between ACEs at Y1 and PSMU at Y3; multiple inferential tests with F-statistics and p-values reported
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Mixed-effects linear modeling examining association between cumulative ACEs score at baseline and PSMU at follow-up; reports coefficient for ACEs → PSMU association
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Reported β = 1.5 for high ACEs vs low ACEs (p < 0.001); also reported β = 1.49 for high ACEs in Table 2
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Reports β=1.5, p<0.001 for cumulative ACEs (≥4) versus low ACEs (<2) predicting higher PSMU
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using mixed-effects linear modeling with secondary ABCD data across 3-year follow-up
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using mixed-effects linear modeling with secondary data from ABCD baseline through 3-year follow-up
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Published journal article in Issues in Mental Health Nursing, 2025; not a preprint or deposit
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Journal article published in Issues in Mental Health Nursing, 2025; DOI: 10.1080/01612840.2025.2559062

title: Digital Media, Genetics, and Risk for ADHD Symptoms in Children: A Longitudinal Study
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Uses Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (ABCD Study), with children from 11,875 enrolled cohort; 8,324 children analyzed at baseline; longitudinal 4-year follow-up
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (data release 5.0); longitudinal cohort comprising 11,875 children born 2005-2009, enrolled at ages 9-10 years from 21 US sites
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Social media use measured via Youth Screen Time Survey (estimated time spent on social media); also measured video games and television separately; social media as distinct exposure variable
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Measures social media use (estimated hours/day on social media) via Youth Screen Time Survey; also measures video games and television/videos separately
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Longitudinal mixed-effects models testing association between social media use and ADHD inattention symptoms; reported β [SE], 0.03 [0.01]; P < .001 for average social media use and inattention symptoms
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Linear mixed models with random intercept and slopes testing social media use × time interaction on ADHD-related symptoms; two-way and three-way interactions tested
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Reported β = 0.03 [SE 0.01] for social media × time; cumulative 4-year effect β = 0.15 [SE 0.03]; P < .001
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Reports social media × time: β [SE], 0.03 [0.01]; P < .001 for inattention symptoms; cumulative 4-year effect β = 0.15 (SE, 0.03; P < .001)
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal cohort study with annual follow-up for 4 years; analyses of associations between digital media use and ADHD symptoms
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis; 4-year follow-up design with mixed-effects modeling examining change in ADHD symptoms over time
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Published journal article in Pediatrics Open Science, 2025; original research article, not preprint
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Journal article published in Pediatrics Open Science, 2026; peer-reviewed original research

title: Concurrent and prospective associations of social media usage with binge eating symptoms in early adolescence.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Data from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study; 1,940 participants aged 11-12 years at Wave 1, analyzed over three annual waves
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (release 5.1, collected September 2016–January 2022); diverse sample of 1,940 participants aged 11-12 years at Wave 1
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Social media addiction (SMA) scores from Cronbach α = 0.84 adapted questionnaire; time spent on social media also measured; both phone/SM-specific variables separately extractable
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Measures social media addiction (SMA) scores via 6-item questionnaire adapted from Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale; also measures time spent on social media (SMT)
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Modified Poisson regression testing prospective associations between SMA and binge eating (BE); reported RR [95% CI] for cross-sectional (Wave 1 & 3) and prospective (Wave 1 → Wave 3) associations
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Modified Poisson regression reporting associations between SMA and binge eating (BE) symptoms at cross-sectional (Wave 1, 3) and prospective (Wave 1 to Wave 3) timepoints
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Reported RR = 2.82 [95% CI 1.61-4.93] for SMA addiction vs no symptoms predicting BE in crude model; Table 3 shows multiple RR estimates with 95% CIs
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Reports addiction SMA group: PR 2.17 (95% CI: 1.22–3.84) at Wave 1, PR 3.45 (95% CI: 1.83–6.49) at Wave 3; prospective RR 1.90 (95% CI: 1.10–3.27); trajectory models: consistently high SMA RR 3.01 (95% CI: 2.07–4.37)
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal and cross-sectional analyses using secondary ABCD data; modified Poisson regression with latent class trajectory modeling
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis; 3-wave design with latent class trajectory modeling and modified Poisson regression

FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Journal article published in Obesity (Silver Spring), 2025; DOI: 10.1002/oby.24199

title: Screen Time and Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder Among Children 9-10Â Years Old: A Prospective Cohort Study.
journal: The Journal of adolescent health : official publication of the Society for Adolescent Medicine

FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of bedtime screen behaviors and sleep disturbance outcomes
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Published journal article in Sleep Health: Journal of the National Sleep Foundation (2023). Not a preprint or duplicate

journal: Journal of child psychology and psychiatry, and allied disciplines
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study (N=11,875), baseline assessment (2016-2018), ages 9-11 years old; 1-year follow-up (2017-2019), ages 10-11 years old
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, baseline 2016-2018 (9-11 years), 1-year follow-up 2017-2019 (10-11 years), n=11,875

FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Modified Poisson regression analyses examining prospective associations between baseline screen time and conduct/oppositional defiant disorder at 1-year follow-up

FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort study analyzing baseline and 1-year follow-up ABCD data
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis examining baseline screen time modalities predicting disruptive behavior disorders at 1-year follow-up

title: Smartphone Use During School Hours by US Youth in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Uses ABCD Study data explicitly: 'EARS (Effortless Assessment Research System) app captures three weeks of passive sensing data on smartphone use in a subsample of adolescents in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study'
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Uses ABCD Study data (N=640 with EARS passive sensing data from follow-up years 4, 5, 6)
FT_IC1_RESOLVE_evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data: N=640 participants with EARS passive sensing data from follow-up years 4, 5, and 6
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Specific smartphone exposure measured via EARS passive sensing: 'smartphone use', 'app categories' (social, games, entertainment, communication, etc.); specifically measures time on Instagram, TikTok, Snapchat and other platform categories
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Measures specific smartphone app use via EARS passive sensing; categorizes apps into 49 categories including social media (Instagram, TikTok, Snapchat) and games
FT_IC2_RESOLVE_evidence: Specific smartphone exposure measured via EARS passive sensing: apps categorized into 49 categories including social media (Instagram, TikTok, Snapchat with 59 social category apps). Platform-specific measurement of smartphone app use during school hours.
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: No inferential statistical test linking smartphone variable to non-SM outcome. Paper is descriptive analysis only: characterizes app usage during school hours with adjusted means (emmeans package) but no regression/correlation with external outcome variables
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Supplementary material shows measurement methods and app categorization but main article text not fully accessible; unclear if reported associations to non-SM outcomes
FT_IC3_RESOLVE_evidence: No inferential statistical test linking smartphone/SM variable to any non-SM outcome. PDF supplement shows descriptive methodology and app categorization. Adjusted means calculated via emmeans package for app usage patterns, but no regression/correlation/association test with external behavioral or clinical outcomes reported.
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Supplementary material indicates eMethods/eReferences present but main PDF shows descriptive statistics (means with 95% CI) for app usage but no coefficient, OR, or p-values for tested associations with outcomes
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Supplementary material references Table I with average times (e.g., medical category 0.13 min) and mentions means, but numeric coefficients/statistics for associations not visible in supplement reviewed
FT_IC4_RESOLVE_evidence: Only descriptive statistics reported (means with 95% CI for app usage times, e.g., medical category 0.13 min, 95% CI 0.11-0.15). No coefficients (β/OR/HR/r/t/χ²) or p-values for tested associations.
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Measurement/descriptive study without external analysis. Paper describes smartphone app usage patterns but reports no empirical analyses linking to behavioral/clinical/non-SM outcomes
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Appears to be empirical analysis of ABCD Study smartphone use patterns but full methods/results not in supplementary material reviewed
FT_IC5_RESOLVE_evidence: Measurement/descriptive study without external analysis. Paper characterizes smartphone app usage patterns and categories but reports no empirical analyses linking phone/SM to behavioral, clinical, or non-SM outcomes.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Journal article (JAMA, 2026); no evidence of preprint or duplicate
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Published article in JAMA (2026), full journal article (not preprint)
FT_IC6_RESOLVE_evidence: Published journal article in JAMA (January 5, 2026); no evidence of preprint, conference abstract, or duplicate.

title: Associations of Screen Time and Physical Activity With Body Mass Index in Early Adolescence: A Prospective Cohort Study.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Prospective ABCD Study Years 2 and 4: 'This study analyzed prospective data from 5356 US adolescents in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Year 2 (2018–2020, ages 11–12) and Year 4 (2020–2022, ages 13–14)'
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Uses ABCD Study data (N=5356 US adolescents, Year 2: 2018-2020, Year 4: 2020-2022)
FT_IC1_RESOLVE_evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data: N=5356 US adolescents, Year 2 (2018-2020, ages 11-12) and Year 4 (2020-2022, ages 13-14)
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Screen time measured as generic 'total recreational screen time' composite (watching TV/videos, playing video games, texting, social media, video chat) without phone/SM-specific breakdown reported separately. Fails FT-IC2 as no phone/SM-specific coefficient extractable; only aggregate composite
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Measures self-reported recreational screen time via ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey, categorized into 0-4h (low), 4-8h (medium), >8h (high) daily. Surveys ask about watching television and videos, playing video games, texting, social media, video chat
FT_IC2_RESOLVE_evidence: Paper measures 'total recreational screen time' composite via ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey asking about watching television/videos, playing video games, texting, social media, and video chat. However, no smartphone-specific or social-media-specific coefficient is reported separately. Only aggregate composite 'total screen time' categorized as 0-4h, 4-8h, >8h daily is analyzed. Per FT-IC2 rule: 'Generic total screen time or STS composite with no phone/SM breakdown = NOT_MET'. E2 edge case: no phone/SM-specific row extractable from composite
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Poisson regression and linear regression: 'high screen time (>8h/day) was associated with higher risk of overweight/obesity (risk ratio [RR], 1.09; 95% CI, 1.02–1.16; p=0.013)' and 'low step count (1000–6000 steps/day) was associated with higher BMI percentile (coefficient [B], 3.27; 95% CI, 1.54–4.99; p<0.001)' linking exposures to BMI outcome
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Poisson regression models examining screen time associations with BMI percentile and overweight/obesity risk. Linear and multivariable regression models tested dose-response relationships. Example: high screen time (>8h/day) associated with overweight/obesity risk (RR 1.09; 95% CI 1.02-1.16; p=0.013)
FT_IC3_RESOLVE_evidence: Paper fails at FT-IC2 criterion. Although Poisson and linear regression models report screen time associations (RR=1.09 for high vs low screen time with overweight/obesity), these statistics apply only to the aggregate composite screen time measure, not to any separately-measured smartphone or social media variable
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: RR 1.09 (95% CI 1.02–1.16, p=0.013) for high screen time; B 3.27 (95% CI 1.54–4.99, p<0.001) for low step count on BMI percentile; multiple p-values and CIs reported in Table 2
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Numeric statistics reported: RR 1.09 (95% CI 1.02-1.16, p=0.013) for high vs low screen time; coefficient B values for BMI percentile; continuous variables also examined with reported p-values
FT_IC4_RESOLVE_evidence: Although numeric statistics are reported (RR=1.09, 95% CI 1.02-1.16, p=0.013; B=3.27, 95% CI 1.54-4.99, p<0.001), these apply to the composite screen time exposure that fails FT-IC2. No extractable numerics for a qualifying smartphone/SM-specific variable
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical prospective analysis: 'prospective data from 5356 US adolescents...Year 2 (2018–2020)...Year 4 (2020–2022)' with Poisson and linear regression models
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis with 2-year follow-up (Year 2 to Year 4)
FT_IC5_RESOLVE_evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis using ABCD Study Years 2 and 4 with 2-year follow-up period; Poisson and linear regression models examining associations between baseline exposures and Year 4 outcomes
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Journal article (Obesity, 2026); no evidence of preprint or duplicate
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Published journal article in Obesity (2026), original research article (not preprint or duplicate)
FT_IC6_RESOLVE_evidence: Published journal article in Obesity (2026); original research article, not a preprint or duplicate

title: Prospective associations between media parenting practices and adolescent video game use.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study: 'We analyzed 7407 adolescents (51.6% male, age: 12.9±0.6 years) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (year 3: 2019–2021 to year 5: 2021–2023)'
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Uses ABCD Study data (N=7407 adolescents, Year 3: 2019-2021 to Year 5: 2021-2023)
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Exposure is video games only, specifically 'video game use' and 'mature-rated video games'. Video games are explicitly listed as NOT_MET per FT-IC2 criteria: 'Console or PC video games' are excluded; 'Video games unspecified platform → NOT_MET (ambiguous)' (rule E17)
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Study measures video game use (mature-rated games, problematic video game use, weekend video game time) but NOT smartphone or social media specifically. Focus is on parental media practices predicting video game behaviors. Does not meet criteria for specific smartphone/SM variable
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Mixed-effects ordinal logistic regression and generalized linear models: 'Greater parental screen time modeling...was associated with higher odds of playing mature-rated video games [adjusted odds ratio (AOR) 1.08, 95% confidence interval (CI) 1.02, 1.14]' and 'Higher mealtime screen use was associated with greater problematic video game use [beta (B) 0.02, 95% CI 0.01, 0.04]'
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Mixed-effects ordinal logistic regression and generalized linear models assess associations between parent media practices and video game behaviors. Example: higher mealtime screen use associated with greater problematic video game use (B 0.02, 95% CI 0.01, 0.04); greater screen time limits associated with lower mature-rated game odds (AOR 0.90, 95% CI 0.86, 0.95)
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: AOR 1.08 (95% CI 1.02, 1.14, p=0.005) for parental screen time modeling; B 0.02 (95% CI 0.01, 0.04, p=0.001) for mealtime screen use on problematic gaming; multiple p-values and CIs in Table 2
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical prospective analysis: 'year 3 (2019–2021)...year 5 (2021–2023)' with mixed-effects ordinal logistic regression and generalized linear models assessing parenting practices predicting video game behaviors
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis with 1- and 2-year follow-up
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Journal article (World Journal of Pediatrics, 2026); no evidence of preprint or duplicate
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Published journal article in World Journal of Pediatrics (2026), original research article

FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Uses ABCD Study (https://abcdstudy.org) with baseline (T0) and 4-year longitudinal follow-up (T1-T4) of 8324 children aged 9-10 years at baseline. Data release 5.0.

Methods state: 'The behavioral data were obtained from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (https://abcdstudy.org/; data release 5.0), a longitudinal cohort comprising 11 875 children born between 2005 and 2009... enrolled at ages 9 to 10 years from 21 data collection sites across the United States between 2016 and 2018.'
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Social media use explicitly measured: time spent on social networking sites (Facebook, Instagram) + video chat + texting via phone/tablet/computer, self-reported hours/day. Separate from video games and TV/videos. Authors categorize DM use: (a) social media use (4+5+6 items combining social networking, texting, video chat), (b) playing video games, (c) watching television/videos. Qualifies as specific smartphone/social media variable per prompt definition (time on phone, apps, social media platform-specific).
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Measures specific digital media types: social media use (texting + social networking sites + video chat), video games, and television/videos. Methods: 'Children provided reports on the number of hours they spent on weekdays and weekends excluding school-related work on the following: (1) watching television or movies; (2) watching videos (eg, YouTube); (3) playing video games... (4) texting... (5) visiting social networking sites... (6) using video chat.' Categorized as: '(a) social media use (4 + 5 + 6), (b) playing video games (3), or (c) watching television/videos (1 + 2).' Qualifies as smartphone/app use.
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Linear mixed models with fixed effects for DM use, time, and interaction terms (DM use × time capturing longitudinal effects; three-way interaction DM use × time × PGS-ADHD testing moderation). Results for social media use and inattention: social media × time: β [SE], 0.03 [0.01]; P < .001. Inferential statistical test (mixed model regression).
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Reports linear mixed models with time × DM use interaction testing longitudinal associations with ADHD-related symptoms. Results: 'Average social media use was associated with increased inattention symptoms over time (β [SE], 0.03 [0.01]; P < .001), with a cumulative 4-year effect of β = 0.15 (SE, 0.03; P < .001).' Also cross-lagged panel models shown in Figure 3.
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Table 2 reports standardized β (standard error) for social media use × time interaction: 0.03 (0.01) for inattention; cumulative 4-year effect: β [SE], 0.15 [0.03]; P < .001. Figure 2 provides quartile-stratified trajectories with numerical values. Cross-lagged panel models (Figure 3) report standardized β coefficients (e.g., social media predicts inattention: β [SE], 0.03 [0.01]; P = .004).
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Multiple numeric values reported: 'β [SE], 0.03 [0.01]; P < .001' for social media use → inattention yearly effect; '4-year effect of β = 0.15 (SE, 0.03; P < .001)'; standardized β weights in Figure 3. P-values, β coefficients, and standard errors throughout.
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis: longitudinal mixed model analysis of 4-year follow-up data examining associations between digital media use and ADHD symptoms with genetic predisposition (polygenic risk scores) as moderator.
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis. Longitudinal cohort followed annually for 4 years (T0-T4) with linear mixed models analyzing DM use → ADHD symptoms associations. Methods: 'Longitudinal associations between average DM use and ADHD-related symptoms were analyzed using separate linear mixed models with random intercept and slopes.'

FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Exposure is 'screen media activity (SMA)' defined as six types: TV watching, video watching, video games, texting, social media, and video chat. Text states 'TV watching predicted higher externalizing problems' and 'video watching predicted increased internalizing' and 'video games.' While social media is measured, the primary results emphasize TV and video (non-phone video watching). No single extractable smartphone/SM-specific coefficient. Mixed-modality paper with only aggregate or non-SM results emphasized.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Measures 'Screen media activity (SMA)' with six component types: television, videos, video games, video chat, social media. Video watching and social media coefficients are separately extractable
FT_IC2_RESOLVE_evidence: Measures Screen Media Activity (SMA) with six measured components: television, video watching, video games, video chat, texting on phone/tablet, and social media (visiting social networking sites). FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Because FT-IC2 is not met for a distinct smartphone/SM variable, FT-IC3 cannot be met. FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Uses linear mixed models, cross-lagged panel models, and mediation analyses. FT_IC3_RESOLVE_evidence: Linear mixed models, cross-lagged panel models, and mediation analyses testing SMA and media-specific associations with mental health outcomes (internalizing, externalizing, stress problems) and brain development. FT_IC4_RESOLVE_evidence: β coefficients with SE and pfdr reported throughout. Text reports: β = 0.030, SE = 0.012, pfdr = 0.016 for SMA-internalizing; TV watching β = 0.054, pfdr < 0.001; video watching β = 0.061, pfdr < 0.001. Mediation effects with β and p-values also reported

Results section includes correlation coefficients (e.g., Table 1 shows bivariate correlations) and pathway coefficients from latent growth curve models. Example: social media hours correlated with cognitive performance measures; model parameters reported in text and tables.
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal ABCD analysis using parallel process latent growth curve models across 4 years with multiple cognitive domains
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using parallel process latent growth curve models examining associations between social media use and cognitive performance trajectories over 4 years in early adolescence.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Research on Adolescence (2025), not a preprint or duplicate
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Research on Adolescence (2025). Not a preprint or duplicate.

title: Neighborhood Quality and Screen Use: Findings From the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data from baseline 2016-2018 cohort. Abstract explicitly states 'Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study' with 8,446 adolescents aged 9-10.
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: N=8,446 adolescents from 2016-2018 baseline with 2 years of follow-up
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Exposes measured are neighborhood quality indices (COI, SVI, ADI). Outcomes are 'daily screen time measures' aggregated as 'total screen time, particularly television/movie watching, video game playing, and video streaming' with NO separate smartphone or social media variable reported. Generic 'total screen time' composite without phone/SM breakdown = NOT_MET per FT-IC2 rule.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Exposure is 'neighborhood quality measures' (COI, SVI, ADI) via geospatial data. Outcome is 'total screen time' generic composite with no smartphone/SM-specific breakdown. Abstract specifies 'television/movie watching, video game playing, and video streaming' but these are grouped in aggregate 'total screen time' measure only.
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Mixed-effects linear regression with neighborhood indices as predictors and screen time as outcome. Reports coefficients: 'compared to highest COI quintile, lower COI quintiles (1st-4th) were associated with higher total screen time (B = 0.39-0.64, P<.05)'. Inferential test present.
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: FT-IC2 fails; paper does not separately report smartphone/SM-specific coefficient
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Numeric values reported: β coefficients (B = 0.39-0.64, 0.28-0.64, 0.46-1.17) and p-values (P<.05, P<.01, P<.001) for associations.
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Fails FT-IC3
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical analysis: prospective cohort study (baseline 2016-2018, 2-year follow-up) using mixed-effects models on ABCD data examining associations.
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Fails FT-IC2
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Published journal article (Academic Pediatrics, 2026). No evidence of prior publication as preprint/conference abstract.
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Journal article, empirical analysis

title: Prospective Associations Between Early Adolescent Problematic Screen Use, Mental Health, Sleep, and Substance Use.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Prospective cohort from ABCD Study (Year 2, 2018-2020; Year 3 follow-up). Paper explicitly states 'Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study' with N=8,119 (ages 11-12 at baseline). Uses U.S. ABCD data.
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: N=8,119 participants Year 2 (2018-2020; ages 11-12), with Year 3 follow-up outcomes. ABCD Study 5.0 release.
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Three QUALIFYING problematic use scales measured at Year 2: (1) Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (MPIQ; 8-question, mean 3.08 SD 1.10), (2) Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ; 6-question, mean 1.84 SD 0.89), and (3) Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (6-question, mean 2.09 SD 1.06). All are specific phone/SM/gaming scales. MPIQ measures 'texting, emailing, video chatting.' Per criteria: MPIQ and SMAQ are qualifying problematic/addictive scales.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Three specific smartphone/SM problematic use scales: (1) Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (8 items, range 1-7), (2) Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (6 items, range 1-6 per Bergen Facebook scale), (3) Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (6 items, range 1-6 per Bergen scale). All are problematic-use addiction-like scales measuring behavioral patterns.
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Multiple linear and logistic regression models reported. Example: 'Problematic mobile phone use and social media use were prospectively associated with higher depressive, somatic, attention/deficit, oppositional defiant, and conduct problems scores' and 'higher odds of developing suicidal behaviors, sleep disturbance, and substance initiation.' Tests include CBCL continuous outcomes (linear regression) and binary outcomes (logistic regression with odds ratios). Figure 1 and 2 display coefficients and ORs.
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Linear and logistic regression models: Problematic phone and SM use prospectively associated with CBCL DSM-oriented scales (continuous); suicidal behaviors, sleep disturbance, substance initiation (binary outcomes). Figures 1 and 2 report forest plots of coefficients and AORs with 95% CIs.
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Extensive numeric reporting in Figures 1-2 and text. Examples from Figure 1: depressive problems β=0.31 (phone), 0.42 (social media), 0.32 (video games); from Figure 2: suicide behaviors OR=1.57 (phone), 1.86 (social media), 1.54 (video games). All with reported p-values and confidence intervals.
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Coefficients reported in Figures 1 and 2 forest plots with 95% CIs and p-values. Abstract states 'B = 0.39-0.64' range examples; text reports linear regression coefficients and AORs. Appendix Table 3 available online with full numeric results.
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis. Year 2 (2018-2020) baseline measures predict Year 3 outcomes 1 year later using linear/logistic regression. Measurement paper with external outcome associations.
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Prospective cohort analysis: exposures measured Year 2 (ages 11-12), outcomes assessed Year 3 (1-year follow-up). Uses linear and logistic regression with covariates; not measurement-only.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Published journal article in American Journal of Preventive Medicine (2026). No indication of prior publication as preprint/duplicate.
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Published journal article (American Journal of Preventive Medicine, 2026)

title: Associations of adolescent social media use trajectories with spatial and verbal memory: a prospective cohort study.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Prospective cohort from ABCD Study (baseline 2016-2018, Year 2 2018-2020). Paper states 'Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study' with N=7,528 (mean age 10 years, 8-13 range). Uses U.S. ABCD data.
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: N=7,528 prospective cohort, baseline ages 8-13 years (2016-2018) to Year 2 follow-up (2018-2020). ABCD 5.1 release.
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Social media use trajectory measured as daily hours spent on social media (Instagram, Facebook, Twitter, etc.) from Youth Screen Time Survey at baseline, Year 1, and Year 2. Weighted average calculated: '([weekday average x 5] + [weekend average x 2])/7'. Specific social media exposure measured separately as continuous variable (hours/day). Explicitly a social media-specific variable.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Independent variable: youth-reported daily social media use measured via Youth Screen Time Survey at baseline, Year 1, Year 2. Platforms specified: Instagram, Facebook, Twitter. Weighted average hours/day calculated from weekday/weekend reports. Specific to social media (not generic screen time).
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Linear regression models examining associations between social media trajectories (3 groups identified via group-based trajectory modeling) and cognitive outcomes at Year 2, adjusted for covariates. Multiple statistical tests reported: 'Linear regression models estimated the association between baseline–Year 2 social media time trajectory and Year 2 cognitive performance scores'. Examples: 'low increasing use associated with lower RAVLT Initial Trials (B: −1.38; 95% CI: −1.82, −0.94; P < 0.001)' and 'high increasing use associated with lower LMT accuracy (B: −0.03; 95% CI: −0.05, −0.01; P = 0.004)'.
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Linear regression models: social media time trajectories (3 groups: no/very low, low-increasing, high-increasing) associated with cognitive outcomes (Little Man Task accuracy, Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test performance at Year 2). Adjusted for baseline cognition, age, sex, race, ethnicity, income, parental education, ADHD symptoms, depression, non-SM screen use, site.
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Extensive numeric reporting. Table 2 and text provide β coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values for all cognitive outcomes. Examples: LMT accuracy (low increasing B=−0.01, high increasing B=−0.03), RAVLT Initial Trials (low increasing B=−1.38, high increasing B=−1.90), RAVLT Retroactive Interference (low increasing B=−0.38, high increasing B=−0.61), RAVLT Long Delay (low increasing B=−0.41, high increasing B=−0.55). All p-values <0.05 reported.
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Regression coefficients with 95% CIs reported: RAVLT Initial Trials (B: -1.38; 95% CI: -1.82, -0.94), RAVLT Retroactive Interference (B: -0.38; 95% CI: -0.52, -0.25), RAVLT Long Delay (B: -0.41; 95% CI: -0.55, -0.26), LMT accuracy (B: -0.03; 95% CI: -0.05, -0.01). All in abstract and main text.
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis using group-based trajectory modeling (GBTM) to identify social media patterns over 2 years, then linear regression to associate trajectories with cognitive outcomes measured at Year 2. Measurement of cognition paired with external outcome (cognitive performance).
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Prospective cohort with trajectory-based modeling: social media use patterns baseline-Year 2, cognitive outcomes measured Year 2. Original empirical analysis using group-based trajectory modeling (GBTM) and linear regression.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Published journal article in Lancet Regional Health - Americas (2026). No evidence of prior publication as preprint/duplicate.
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Published journal article (Lancet Regional Health - Americas, 2026)

title: Differential Relationships of Child Anxiety and Depression to Child Report and Parent Report of Electronic Media Use.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: '9- to 11-year-olds from the 21-site adolescent brain cognitive development study'
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data. Paper states: 'Data from a sample of 4524 children in the ABCD study were used' and collected from '21-site adolescent brain cognitive development study' (Methods, Participants section).
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Measures multiple SM/phone variables: 'television/movies, watching videos (e.g., YouTube), playing video games, texting, visiting social networking sites (e.g., Twitter, Instagram), and using video chat (e.g., FaceTime)' plus 'mature-rated video games' and 'R-rated movies'
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Measures specific smartphone/electronic media variables. Paper measures: TV shows/movies, videos, video games, texting, social networks, video chatting (specific types of electronic media use). ABCD Screen Time Questionnaire (STQ) for children measures different forms of electronic media. Specific phone/SM-related variables are separately measured: 'video gaming and video chatting related to anxiety' and 'video watching related to depression' (Abstract).
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Reports inferential tests with ST variables: 'generalized linear mixed models' testing associations between ST types/content and behavioral outcomes (CBCL syndrome scales)
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Reports inferential statistical tests linking electronic media variables to non-SM outcomes (anxiety and depression). Paper uses 'generalized linear mixed models' and reports associations. For example: 'child report of weekday EMU predicting anxiety' with t-values and p-values reported (Table 2: t=3.309, p=0.001). Tests include relationships between specific EM types and psychological outcomes.
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Reports multiple statistics: IRR values (e.g., 'IRR = 1.059' for TV/movies→rule-breaking), β coefficients, p-values, and 95% CIs throughout Tables 2-3
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Numeric statistics reported throughout. Table 2 reports coefficients, t-values, significance levels, and 95% CIs for multiple EM-outcome associations. Example: 'Child reported weekday use' coefficient=0.055, t=3.309, p=0.001, CI [0.022-0.088]. Table 4 reports additional coefficients for specific EM types.
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional observational study using baseline ABCD data with formal statistical modeling
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis. Cross-sectional study examining relationships between baseline electronic media use and anxiety/depression. Paper conducted 'generalized linear mixed models' with multiple analyses examining phone/SM variables in relation to non-SM psychological outcomes.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Journal article (peer-reviewed): published in International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity, 2019
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Published journal article in 'Child Psychiatry & Human Development' (2019), original empirical research not a preprint, protocol, or duplicate.

title: 24-Hour Movement Behaviors and Impulsivity.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Uses ABCD Study: 'first annual curated release of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study. Participants included 4524 children between the ages of 8 and 11 years'
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Paper explicitly states 'Data from this study were part of the first annual curated release of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study. Participants included 4524 children between the ages of 8 and 11 years.' ABCD is the U.S. ABCD Study.
FT_IC1_RESOLVE_evidence: Uses ABCD Study first annual curated release with 4524 children aged 8-11 years from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study.
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Measures recreational screen time (ST) as SM/phone variable: 'Canadian 24-Hour Movement Guidelines for Children and Youth...≤2 hours of recreational screen time (ST) per day'
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Screen time (ST) measured as recreational screen time via Youth Screen Time Survey (12 items), encompassing watching shows and movies, watching videos, video games, texting, and other digital activities on weekday and weekend days.
FT_IC2_RESOLVE_evidence: Measures specific recreational screen time (ST) variable as part of Canadian 24-Hour Movement Guidelines (≤2 hours of ST per day). ST is separately measured via Youth Screen Time Survey (12 items) encompassing television, videos, video games, texting, and other digital activities on weekday and weekend days. ST is extractable as distinct exposure variable.
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Reports inferential statistics linking ST recommendations to impulsivity outcomes: 'Meeting the ST and sleep recommendations was associated with less impulsive behaviors on all dimensions' with CIs and p-values reported
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Multiple inferential tests reported linking ST and movement behavior combinations to impulsivity dimensions. Example: Meeting ST + sleep recommendations was negatively associated with positive urgency (95% CI: -0.20 to -0.10).
FT_IC3_RESOLVE_evidence: Multiple inferential tests reported linking ST (as component of movement behavior recommendations) to impulsivity dimensions. Example: 'Meeting the ST and sleep recommendations was associated with less impulsive behaviors on all dimensions of impulsivity' with CIs and p-values reported in abstract and tables.
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Reports extractable statistics: 95% confidence intervals and coefficients throughout results (e.g., 'negative urgency (95% CI: −0.20 to −0.10)'; Table 3 contains standardized β coefficients and p-values)
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Numeric coefficients reported throughout. Example: β = -0.12 (p<0.001) for ST + sleep recommendations vs positive urgency; β = -0.09 (95% CI: -0.12 to -0.05) for ST + sleep vs positive urgency.
FT_IC4_RESOLVE_evidence: Table 3 reports standardized β coefficients and p-values. Example: 'Only ST: β=-0.08, p<.001' (negative urgency); 'PA + ST: β=-0.05, p=.002' (negative urgency); numeric CIs and SEs reported throughout tables.
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional observational study examining associations between movement behavior recommendations and impulsivity dimensions
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Cross-sectional empirical analysis of ABCD baseline data examining associations between 24-hour movement behaviors (including ST) and impulsivity dimensions using structural equation modeling.
FT_IC5_RESOLVE_evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional observational study using structural equation modeling examining associations between adherence to 24-hour movement behavior recommendations (including ST) and dimensions of impulsivity.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Journal article: published in Pediatrics, 2019
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Pediatrics, 2019, with distinct analyses from other ABCD studies.
FT_IC6_RESOLVE_evidence: Published journal article: Guerrero MD, et al. 24-Hour Movement Behaviors and Impulsivity. Pediatrics. 2019;144(3):e20190187.

title: Screen time and problem behaviors in children: exploring the mediating role of sleep duration.
journal: The international journal of behavioral nutrition and physical activity
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Uses ABCD baseline data, N ~11,875 children, US 21-site cohort.
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Uses ABCD Study. Methods state: 'Parents and children provided cross-sectional baseline data (2016–18) as part of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study, a broadly US representative sample of 11,875 children aged 9 to 10 years.'
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Measures separate screen time modalities including TV/movies, videos, video games, social media, video chat/texting with extractable modality-specific coefficients.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Measures specific screen time types: television/movies, videos, video games, and social media (texting, social networking sites, video chat collapsed). Methods: 'Children self-reported their ST behavior, which comprised ST types (television/movies, videos, video games, and social media) and content (mature-rated video games and R-rated movies).' Qualifies as smartphone/app use (texting, social media, video chat).
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Negative binomial SEM with mediation linking screen type/content to behavioral problems (CBCL); reports IRR with 95% CIs.
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Reports negative binomial SEM with mediation analysis. Tests ST types and content associations with CBCL problem behaviors via sleep duration pathway. Multiple inferential tests reported: direct effects via Mplus MLR, mediation effects via bootstrapping. Example: 'watching television/movies was associated with a 5.9% increase in rule-breaking behavior (IRR = 1.059)'.
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Multiple IRRs with 95% CIs reported per screen modality-behavior association (e.g., social media IRR linked to externalizing behaviors).
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Multiple numeric values reported: IRRs with examples 'IRR = 1.059' for TV/movies → rule-breaking, 'IRR = 1.050' for TV/movies → social problems, 'IRRs ranging from .834 to .905' for sleep duration effects. All values in text and tables.
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD data using SEM.
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis. Cross-sectional baseline data with mediation analysis testing indirect pathways between ST behaviors and behavioral syndromes through sleep duration. Methods: 'Study hypotheses were tested using Mplus Version 8.2. Using the MODEL INDIRECT command in Mplus, we examined the mediating role of sleep duration.'
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article.
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Published journal article in International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity, 2019. No evidence of duplicate.

title: Social epidemiology of early adolescent problematic screen use in the United States.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Uses ABCD Study Year 2 follow-up (2018-2020), N=8753 children aged 10-14 years
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study Year 2 data (2018-2020), N=8753 adolescents aged 10-14 years
FT_IC1_RESOLVE_evidence: Uses ABCD Study Year 2 (2018-2020), N=8753 children aged 10-14 years
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Measures three specific SM/phone variables: Video Game Addiction Questionnaire, Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ), Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (MPIQ)
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Measures three specific smartphone/social media variables: Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ), Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ), and Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (MPIQ)
FT_IC2_RESOLVE_evidence: Measures three specific SM/phone variables: Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ), Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ), Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (MPIQ). All are problematic-use scales qualifying under FT-IC2.
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses linking problematic phone/SM use to sociodemographic factors (age, sex, race/ethnicity, income, parental education). Table 3 reports associations with all three screen use measures
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses examining associations between sociodemographic factors and problematic screen use across three modalities
FT_IC3_RESOLVE_evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses linking problematic phone/SM use (DVs) to sociodemographic factors (IVs). Table 3 reports associations across all three screen use measures with test statistics.
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Table 3 reports B coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values for all associations (e.g., Male vs Female: B=0.74, 95% CI 0.69-0.79, p<0.001 for video games)
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Table 3 reports β coefficients with 95% confidence intervals (e.g., males vs females: B = 0.74 (0.69 to 0.79) for video games, p<0.001; B = -0.11 (-0.16 to -0.05) for social media, p<0.001)
FT_IC4_RESOLVE_evidence: Table 3 reports B coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values (e.g., Male vs Female: B=0.74, 95% CI 0.69-0.79, p<0.001 for video games; B=-0.11, 95% CI -0.16 to -0.05, p<0.001 for social media)
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD data with hypothesis testing and reported associations
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD Study using multiple linear regression to estimate associations
FT_IC5_RESOLVE_evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD Year 2 data with hypothesis testing and reported associations across three problematic use measures
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Published journal article (Pediatric Research, 2022); no evidence of duplicate/preprint
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Published journal article in Pediatric Research (2022), no duplicate evidence
FT_IC6_RESOLVE_evidence: Published journal article (Pediatric Research, 2022); no evidence of duplicate/preprint

title: Parental psychological problems were associated with higher screen time and the use of mature-rated media in children.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Uses ABCD Study baseline data (2016-2018), N=10,650 children aged 9.9±0.6 years
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, N=10,650 children (5,112 girls, 5,538 boys) aged 9.9±0.6 years, cross-sectional baseline assessment
FT_IC1_RESOLVE_evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study baseline data (2016-2018), N=10,650 children aged 9.9±0.6 years, recruited via probability sampling from USA population
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Paper measures general screen time categories (TV, videos, video games, social media, mature-rated media) but does NOT measure a SPECIFIC smartphone or social media variable as the primary exposure. Measures generic screen time across modalities without phone-specific or SM-specific metrics. Generic total screen time composite without phone/SM breakdown.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Measures specific screen media variables: TV watching, video watching (YouTube), online games, social media (Twitter, Instagram), mature-rated video games, mature-rated movies. Operationalized via 7-point Likert scale for daily usage hours.
FT_IC2_RESOLVE_evidence: Paper measures specific screen media variables separately: TV watching, video watching (e.g., YouTube), playing online games, texting, social media (Twitter, Instagram), video chat (Facetime), and mature-rated games/movies. Assessed via Youth Screen Time Survey with 7-point Likert scale (none, <30 min, 30 min, 1h, 2h, 3h, ≥4h) for both weekday and weekend days. Per FT-IC2 qualifying criteria: platform-specific social media (Twitter, Instagram) and age of acquisition of phone/app use measured.
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Screen time is dependent variable, not independent variable. Parental psychological problems are predictors. No smartphone/SM variable is linked to a non-SM outcome as required by FT-IC3.
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Reports multilevel general linear models (for continuous screen time in minutes/day) and multilevel logistic regression models (for meeting vs not meeting ≤2h/day recommendation). Tests associations between parental psychological problems (predictor) and children's screen time/media behaviors (outcomes).
FT_IC3_RESOLVE_evidence: Multilevel general linear models and multilevel logistic regression testing associations between parental psychological problems (externalizing, internalizing, total) and children's screen time outcomes. Per Methods: 'Multilevel general linear models to examine if presence of parental psychological problems was associated with children's daily screen time, measured as minutes per day. Multilevel logistic regression models were used to examine if presence of parental psychological problems was associated with the binary outcome of children meeting versus not meeting the recommendation of two daily hours or less.' Results section reports regression coefficients and ORs across multiple screen media types (TV, videos, games, social media).
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: FT-IC2 failure prevents scoring. Screen time is outcome, not exposure variable.
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Reports OR, 95% CI, and regression coefficients: 'Parental internalising problems were associated with children watching mature-rated movies (OR=1.14, 95% CI=1.00, 1.30) and playing mature-rated games (OR=1.27, 95% CI=1.11, 1.45)'
FT_IC4_RESOLVE_evidence: Numeric values reported for TV, videos, games, and mature-rated media outcomes. Examples from Table 4: 'Parental internalising problems were associated with children watching mature-rated movies (OR = 1.14, 95% CI = 1.00, 1.30) and playing mature-rated games (OR = 1.27, 95% CI = 1.11, 1.45).' Table 2 reports coefficients with 95% CIs for total screen time minutes per day.
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis with inferential statistics (multilevel regression, logistic regression)
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD baseline data examining associations between parental psychological problems and children's screen media behaviors
FT_IC5_RESOLVE_evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD baseline data (2016-2018) with inferential statistics (multilevel regression, logistic regression). Per Methods: 'We used multilevel general linear models to examine if presence of parental psychological problems was associated with children's daily screen time' and 'We used logistic regression analyses to examine if presence of parental psychological problem was associated with children's use of mature-rated games (no/yes) or movies (no/yes).'
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Published journal article (Acta Paediatrica, 2022); no evidence of duplicate
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Published journal article in Acta Paediatrica, 2022
FT_IC6_RESOLVE_evidence: Published peer-reviewed journal article in Acta Paediatrica (2022), DOI 10.1111/apa.16253; no evidence of duplicate/preprint status

title: One-year predictions of delayed reward discounting in the adolescent brain cognitive development study.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Uses ABCD Study baseline data, N=4042 with follow-up at 1 year; assessed at ages 9-10 years
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, baseline N=4,042 (from ABCD data release 2.0.1 with 11,875 participants at baseline)
FT_IC1_RESOLVE_evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study baseline data, N = 4,042 (from ABCD data release 2.0.1); baseline ages 9–10 years; 1-year follow-up sample available
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Includes 'screen media activity' as one of 131 questionnaire/task predictor variables in machine learning models. Measures typical weekly screen time on computers, cellphones, tablets, and electronic devices (watching TV, videos, gaming, texting, social media, video chatting).
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Screen media activity is listed as a KEY PREDICTIVE VARIABLE in the abstract/results but is NOT the exposure/outcome under primary investigation. The PRIMARY outcome is Delayed Reward Discounting (DRD), a cognitive decision-making measure. Screen time serves as a COVARIATE/PREDICTOR in machine learning models predicting DRD. Fail: smartphone/SM is neither IV nor DV in the hypothesized association of interest.
FT_IC2_RESOLVE_evidence: Screen media activity is listed as ONE OF 131 questionnaire/task predictor variables in machine learning models but does NOT have a separately reported coefficient. The paper identifies 'screen media activity' as a 'key predictive variable' in the abstract and states it was among 'neuropsychological test performance, socioeconomic status, screen media activity, psychopathology, parenting, and personality' predictive factors. However, NO SPECIFIC NUMERIC VALUE (β, coefficient, importance score, SHAP value) is reported for screen media activity itself. Per E4: 'ML — coefficient reported → MET. SHAP/Gini/permutation only → NOT_MET.' Screen media is a feature in an ML ensemble without extractable coefficient.
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Machine learning predictive models with cross-validation test screen media activity and other variables as predictors of delayed reward discounting (DRD) at 1-year follow-up. Models tested multiple algorithms (elastic net, random forest, light gradient boosting, support vector regression).
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Not applicable: FT_IC2 not met. No inferential test specifically linking smartphone/SM variable to a non-SM outcome.
FT_IC3_RESOLVE_evidence: Not applicable: FT_IC2 not met. No inferential test specifically links screen media activity (as a reported coefficient) to delayed reward discounting.

FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Mixed-effect models test associations between social media use and video gaming with sleep outcomes (time in bed, bedtimes, sleep onset latency)

FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Numeric values reported; associations between screen time variables and sleep behaviors with statistical significance and effect estimates
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis; mixed-effect models examining associations between screen time changes across pandemic timepoints and sleep behavior changes
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis using mixed-effect longitudinal models examining pre-pandemic baseline and pandemic timepoints
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Published journal article in Health Psychology 2023
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Published journal article in Health Psychology, 2023; original peer-reviewed empirical work

title: Cyberbullying Victimisation Was Associated With Greater Manic Symptoms in Early Adolescence: A Prospective Cohort Study.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Uses ABCD Study data from period 2 follow-up (2018-2020) to period 3 follow-up (2019-2021) with 9,095 adolescents aged 10-13 years

FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Measures cyberbullying (lifetime and past 12-month) as exposure variable. Cyberbullying alone without separately measured phone/SM variable fails E6.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Primary exposure is cyberbullying victimization (lifetime and past 12-month), not a smartphone/SM-specific variable. FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Cannot assess; FT-IC2 failed. Primary analysis examines cyberbullying→manic symptoms, not phone/SM→outcome association
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Numeric coefficients, confidence intervals, and p-values reported in Table 2 and text
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Cannot assess; FT-IC2 failed. Coefficients reported are for cyberbullying (β=0.41, p=0.001) not phone/SM

FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical prospective/longitudinal analysis using multivariable linear regression with ABCD data
